issues.original: This is the first in a six-part field guide to Claude Code. The order is deliberate: each piece unlocks the next. By the end you will be using six features 90% of users never touch.
issues.fixed: This is the first in a six-part field guide to Claude Code. The order is deliberate: each piece unlocks the next. By the end, you'll be using six features that 90% of users never touch.

issues.original: There is one supported install path, and it's the right one:
issues.fixed: There is one supported install path, and it's the right one.

issues.original: The login flow opens a browser, you authorize the CLI, and you're done. Your auth token lives in [CODE]. There is no API key in [CODE] to leak — that's worth noting because most 'AI CLI' tools get this wrong.
issues.fixed: The login flow opens a browser, you authorize the CLI, and you're done. Your auth token lives in [CODE]. No API key in [CODE] to leak — that's worth noting because most 'AI CLI' tools get this wrong.

issues.fixed: On Windows Subsystem for Linux, the browser-based login flow can't open a browser automatically. The CLI will print a URL. Copy it, open it in your Windows browser, authorize, and the CLI will pick up the token. It works, but not smoothly.

issues.original: Claude will use its own tooling to [CODE], summarize, and reply. If that worked, the install is done.
issues.fixed: Claude will use its own tooling to [CODE], summarize, and reply. If that works, the install is done.

issues.original: You can also run Claude Code non-interactively from scripts:
issues.fixed: You can also run Claude Code non-interactively from scripts.

issues.original: The [CODE] flag (print mode) skips the interactive loop. This is useful for scripts, CI pipelines, and quick one-off questions where you don't want a full session.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] flag (print mode) skips the interactive loop. This is useful for scripts, CI pipelines, and quick one-off questions where you don't need a full session.

issues.original: This is the part most users never read. Claude Code merges configuration from three locations, in order of increasing precedence:
issues.fixed: This is the part most users never read. Claude Code merges configuration from three locations, in order of increasing precedence.

issues.original: Why three layers and not two? Some settings are personal and global (like your default model and editor command), some are team and shared (like the linter and test command), and some are personal but project-scoped (like your API key for the staging server).
issues.fixed: Why three layers and not two? Some settings are personal and global (like your default model and editor command), some are team and shared (like the linter and test command), and some are personal but project-scoped (like your API key for the staging server).

issues.original: The merge is a deep merge with later layers winning. If your machine config sets [CODE] and the project config doesn't mention [CODE] at all, you get Opus. If the project config sets [CODE], the project wins — unless your local config overrides it again.
issues.fixed: The merge is a deep merge with later layers taking precedence. If your machine config sets [CODE] and the project config doesn't mention [CODE] at all, you get Opus. If the project config sets [CODE], the project wins — unless your local config overrides it again.

issues.original: For the [CODE] object, arrays are concatenated. If the project allows [CODE] and your machine config allows [CODE], you get both. Deny rules always take precedence over allow rules regardless of layer.
issues.fixed: For the [CODE] object, arrays are concatenated. If the project allows [CODE] and your machine config allows [CODE], you get both. Deny rules always take precedence over allow rules, regardless of layer.

issues.original: Those are read-only commands I never want to be prompted for. Every time Claude asks 'can I run [CODE]?' it interrupts my flow for zero safety benefit. Put read-only operations here and forget about them.
issues.fixed: Those are read-only commands I never want to be prompted for. Every time Claude asks 'can I run [CODE]?' it interrupts my flow without any safety benefit. Put read-only operations here and forget about them.

issues.original: I don't put anything that writes files, runs tests, or executes project-specific commands here. Those belong in the project layer.
issues.fixed: I don't put anything that writes files, runs tests, or executes project-specific commands here. Those belong in the project layer.

issues.original: This is [CODE]. It gets committed to git. Everyone on the team inherits it.
issues.fixed: This is [CODE]. It gets committed to git, and everyone on the team inherits it.

issues.original: The deny list is as important as the allow list. [CODE] in a Claude session would be catastrophic. [CODE] against production is not something you want an AI to do autonomously. Be explicit about what's off-limits.
issues.fixed: The deny list is as important as the allow list. [CODE] in a Claude session would be catastrophic. [CODE] against production is not something you want an AI to do autonomously. Be explicit about what's off-limits.

issues.original: Hooks let you run shell commands before or after Claude uses specific tools. The [CODE] hook fires before a tool runs, [CODE] after. You can use them for logging, notifications, or validation.
issues.fixed: Hooks let you run shell commands before or after Claude uses specific tools. The [CODE] hook fires before a tool runs, and [CODE] after. You can use them for logging, notifications, or validation.

issues.original: Environment variables set here are available to Claude's shell commands in that project. This is the right place for API keys, database URLs, and other sensitive, project-specific data.
issues.fixed: Environment variables set here are available to Claude's shell commands in that project. This is the right place for API keys, database URLs, and other sensitive, project-specific data.

issues.original: A common mistake is putting sensitive values in the project-level config and committing them. The three-layer system exists to prevent this.
issues.fixed: A common mistake is putting sensitive values in the project-level config and committing them. The three-layer system prevents this.

issues.fixed: After creating the command, you need to restart Claude Code (one of the few places it isn't hot-reloaded).

issues.fixed: Project commands take precedence over global commands with the same name. This means a team can override your personal [CODE] with a project-specific version.

issues.fixed: The command content is treated as a regular prompt. This means:

issues.fixed: Names become the slash command, so keep them short and clear:

issues.original: This pattern — "if arguments exist, scope to them; otherwise, do everything" — makes commands more flexible without needing separate commands for the general and specific cases.
issues.fixed: This pattern — "if arguments exist, scope to them; otherwise, do everything" — makes commands more flexible without needing separate commands for general and specific cases.

issues.fixed: Each command file is a runnable specification of a workflow, which is more useful than a wiki page because it's always up to date (if it weren't, people would fix it because they use it every day).

issues.original: If MCP is how Claude reaches out, hooks are how you reach in. They enforce the rules you care about, not just hope for them.
issues.fixed: If MCP is how Claude reaches out, hooks are how you reach in. They enforce the rules you care about, not just what you hope for.

issues.original: A hook is a shell command that Claude Code runs at specific moments. The two you'll use most:
issues.fixed: A hook is a shell command that Claude Code runs at specific moments. The two you'll use most are:

issues.original: There are others ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]). For day-to-day work, those two are 90% of the value.
issues.fixed: There are others ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]). For day-to-day work, those two provide 90% of the value.

issues.original: Let's walk through what happens when Claude calls a tool and hooks are configured. Understanding this lifecycle is key to writing effective hooks.
issues.fixed: Let's walk through what happens when Claude calls a tool and hooks are configured. Understanding this lifecycle is key to writing effective hooks.

issues.original: When Claude decides to call a tool, this sequence runs:
issues.fixed: When Claude decides to call a tool, this sequence runs:

issues.original: Claude Code resolves the tool name and input arguments.
issues.fixed: Claude Code resolves the tool name and input arguments.

issues.original: It checks the [CODE] hooks in settings. Each hook with a matching [CODE] is selected.
issues.fixed: It checks the [CODE] hooks in settings. Each hook with a matching [CODE] is selected.

issues.original: Selected hooks run **sequentially**, in the order they appear in the config.
issues.fixed: Selected hooks run **sequentially**, in the order they appear in the config.

issues.original: Each hook receives the tool call details as JSON on **stdin**.
issues.fixed: Each hook receives the tool call details as JSON on **stdin**.

issues.original: If **any** hook exits with a non-zero code, the tool call is **blocked**. The hook's stderr output is sent back to the model as an error message.
issues.fixed: If **any** hook exits with a non-zero code, the tool call is **blocked**. The hook's stderr output is sent back to the model as an error message.

issues.original: If all hooks exit with code 0, the tool call proceeds.
issues.fixed: If all hooks exit with code 0, the tool call proceeds.

issues.original: After a tool completes:
issues.fixed: After a tool completes:

issues.original: Claude Code collects the tool's output.
issues.fixed: Claude Code collects the tool's output.

issues.original: It checks [CODE] hooks with matching matchers.
issues.fixed: It checks [CODE] hooks with matching matchers.

issues.original: Selected hooks run sequentially.
issues.fixed: Selected hooks run sequentially.

issues.original: Each hook receives the tool output as JSON on stdin.
issues.fixed: Each hook receives the tool output as JSON on stdin.

issues.original: If a hook writes to stdout, that output **replaces** what the model sees. This is how you can filter, redact, or annotate tool output.
issues.fixed: If a hook writes to stdout, that output **replaces** what the model sees. This is how you can filter, redact, or annotate tool output.

issues.original: Exit codes are informational — a non-zero exit doesn't undo the tool call. But stderr output is logged and can be useful for debugging.
issues.fixed: Exit codes are informational — a non-zero exit doesn't undo the tool call. But stderr output is logged and can be useful for debugging.

issues.original: Runs before the user's prompt is sent to the model. Use it to:
issues.fixed: Runs before the user's prompt is sent to the model. Use it to:

issues.original: Warn about prompts that contain sensitive data
issues.fixed: Warn about prompts that contain sensitive data

issues.original: Add standard context to every prompt
issues.fixed: Add standard context to every prompt

issues.original: Log prompts for audit
issues.fixed: Log prompts for audit

issues.original: Runs when Claude finishes its response and is about to yield control back to the user. Useful for:
issues.fixed: Runs when Claude finishes its response and is about to yield control back to the user. Useful for:

issues.original: Generating summary reports
issues.fixed: Generating summary reports

issues.original: Cleaning up temporary files
issues.fixed: Cleaning up temporary files

issues.original: Sending notifications ("Claude finished the task")
issues.fixed: Sending notifications ("Claude finished the task")

issues.original: Runs when Claude Code sends a desktop notification (e.g., when a long task completes). Use it to route notifications to other channels like Slack, email, or webhooks.
issues.fixed: Runs when Claude Code sends a desktop notification (e.g., when a long task completes). Use it to route notifications to other channels like Slack, email, or webhooks.

issues.original: Runs when a sub-agent (spawned by the main agent for parallel tasks) completes. Use it for logging or aggregating results from parallel work.
issues.fixed: Runs when a sub-agent (spawned by the main agent for parallel tasks) completes. Use it for logging or aggregating results from parallel work.

issues.original: A few critical details about how hooks actually run: *Exit-code semantics differ between PreToolUse and PostToolUse.*
issues.fixed: A few critical details about how hooks actually run: *Exit-code semantics differ between PreToolUse and PostToolUse.*

issues.original: **Hooks are shell commands, not scripts.** The [CODE] field is passed to the system shell ([CODE]). This means you can use shell features like pipes and redirects directly:
issues.fixed: **Hooks are shell commands, not scripts.** The [CODE] field is passed to the system shell ([CODE]). This means you can use shell features like pipes and redirects directly:

issues.original: But for anything beyond a one-liner, use a script file.
issues.fixed: But for anything beyond a one-liner, use a script file.

issues.original: **Hooks have a timeout.** By default, hooks must complete within a few seconds. A hook that hangs will be killed and the tool call proceeds (or is blocked, depending on the hook type). Don't make HTTP calls in hooks unless you set a tight timeout.
issues.fixed: **Hooks have a timeout.** By default, hooks must complete within a few seconds. A hook that hangs will be killed, and the tool call proceeds (or is blocked, depending on the hook type). Don't make HTTP calls in hooks unless you set a tight timeout.

issues.original: **Hooks run synchronously.** Each hook must finish before the next one starts. Five hooks at 200ms each add up to a full second of delay per tool call. Keep hooks fast.
issues.fixed: **Hooks run synchronously.** Each hook must finish before the next one starts. Five hooks at 200ms each add up to a full second of delay per tool call. Keep hooks fast.

issues.original: **Hooks inherit the Claude Code process environment.** They have access to your shell's environment variables, PATH, and so on. But they don't run in your interactive shell — no shell aliases, no [CODE] functions.
issues.fixed: **Hooks inherit the Claude Code process environment.** They have access to your shell's environment variables, PATH, and so on. But they don't run in your interactive shell — no shell aliases, no [CODE] functions.

issues.original: **Stdin is consumed once.** If you have multiple hooks for the same event and matcher, each gets its own copy of stdin. You don't need to worry about one hook consuming the input.
issues.fixed: **Stdin is consumed once.** If you have multiple hooks for the same event and matcher, each gets its own copy of stdin. You don't need to worry about one hook consuming the input.

issues.original: In [CODE] (or its local variant). The minimal example:
issues.fixed: In [CODE] (or its local variant). The minimal example:

issues.original: The [CODE] decides which tools the hook applies to. [CODE] matches the built-in Bash tool. [CODE] matches file writes. [CODE] matches all Playwright MCP tools. Regex is supported — wildcards are common.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] decides which tools the hook applies to. [CODE] matches the built-in Bash tool. [CODE] matches file writes. [CODE] matches all Playwright MCP tools. Regex is supported — wildcards are common.

issues.original: The hook command receives the tool's input as JSON on stdin and gets the conversation context as environment variables. The simplest possible hook is one that reads stdin, decides, and exits with the appropriate code.
issues.fixed: The hook command receives the tool's input as JSON on stdin and gets the conversation context as environment variables. The simplest possible hook reads stdin, decides, and exits with the appropriate code.

issues.original: The matcher field is a regex tested against the tool name. Here's a reference:
issues.fixed: The matcher field is a regex tested against the tool name. Here's a reference:

issues.original: Use the most specific matcher possible. A [CODE] hook runs on every single tool call — including reads, which happen dozens of times per session. That adds up.
issues.fixed: Use the most specific matcher possible. A [CODE] hook runs on every single tool call — including reads, which happen dozens of times per session. That adds up.

issues.original: Register it: [CODE] matcher [CODE]. Block exits with code 1; the message on stderr goes back to the model so it knows why and can adapt. This is the difference between "agent crashes silently" and "agent learns to ask permission."
issues.fixed: Register it: [CODE] matcher [CODE]. Block exits with code 1; the message on stderr goes back to the model so it knows why and can adapt. This is the difference between "agent crashes silently" and "agent learns to ask permission."

issues.original: When a PreToolUse hook blocks a tool call, the model receives the stderr message as an error. A well-written error message helps the model recover:
issues.fixed: When a PreToolUse hook blocks a tool call, the model receives the stderr message as an error. A well-written error message helps the model recover:

issues.original: All examples assume Node 18+, save scripts to [CODE], mark them [CODE], and wire them in [CODE] like:
issues.fixed: All examples assume Node 18+. Save scripts to [CODE], mark them [CODE], and wire them in [CODE] like:

issues.original: *Each hook is a script that reads a JSON payload from stdin and signals back via exit code (verdict) and stderr (explanation). The matcher in settings.json determines which hooks handle each tool call.*
issues.fixed: *Each hook is a script that reads a JSON payload from stdin and signals back with an exit code (verdict) and stderr (explanation). The matcher in settings.json determines which hooks handle each tool call.*

issues.original: The single highest-ROI hook. Stops [CODE] and [CODE] from touching [CODE], [CODE], [CODE].
issues.fixed: The single highest-ROI hook. It stops [CODE] and [CODE] from accessing [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE].

issues.original: The regex list is short on purpose. Long blocklists get ignored when they cause false positives. A blocklist with 50 rules will inevitably block [CODE] and you will disable the whole hook in frustration.
issues.fixed: The regex list is short on purpose. Long blocklists get ignored if they cause false positives. A blocklist with 50 rules will inevitably block [CODE], leading to frustration and disabling the whole hook.

issues.original: I run the blacklist on dev machines and the whitelist on anything production-adjacent.
issues.fixed: I use the blacklist on dev machines and the whitelist on anything production-adjacent.

issues.original: *The same exit code means different things depending on the lifecycle phase. Exit 2 only blocks in PreToolUse; in PostToolUse, the side-effect has already occurred.*
issues.fixed: *The same exit code means different things depending on the lifecycle phase. Exit 2 only blocks in PreToolUse. In PostToolUse, the side-effect has already occurred.*

issues.original: PostToolUse runs *after* the edit. Exit code 2 does not roll anything back — the side-effect already happened. Using exit 1 would surface the error to the model, which might then try to 'fix' a formatting issue by re-editing the file, creating a loop. For formatting, just log the warning and move on.
issues.fixed: PostToolUse runs *after* the edit. Exit code 2 does not roll anything back because the side-effect has already happened. Using exit 1 would surface the error to the model, which might then try to 'fix' a formatting issue by re-editing the file, creating a loop. For formatting, just log the warning and move on.

issues.original: You won't look at this file every day, but when you do, you'll be glad it exists.
issues.fixed: You won't look at this file every day, but when you do, you'll be glad it's there.

issues.original: PreToolUse on [CODE]. Forces the model to read a file before editing it.
issues.fixed: PreToolUse on [CODE]. This forces the model to read a file before editing it.

issues.original: *The hook keeps a small [CODE] map of when each file was last Read. Edits to UNSEEN or STALE files are blocked with a stderr message that tells Claude exactly how to recover.*
issues.fixed: *The hook keeps a small [CODE] map of when each file was last read. Edits to unseen or stale files are blocked with a stderr message that tells Claude exactly how to recover.*

issues.original: After hooks, the next thing that changes how Claude Code feels is *concurrency control*. Not in the threading sense, but in terms of how many tasks the model handles, their isolation, and the level of oversight.
issues.fixed: After hooks, the next thing that changes how Claude Code operates is *concurrency control*. Not in the threading sense, but in terms of how many tasks it handles, their isolation, and the level of oversight.

issues.original: Three features, in escalating order of trust required.
issues.fixed: Three features, in increasing order of trust required.

issues.original: Plan mode is the cheapest of the three. Press [CODE] until the indicator says **plan**. The model now plans without taking any actions. It will read, think, propose, and stop. You read the plan. You either approve, edit, or kill it. Only then does it execute.
issues.fixed: Plan mode is the cheapest of the three. Press [CODE] until the indicator says **plan**. The model now plans without taking any actions. It reads, thinks, proposes, and stops. You read the plan, then either approve, edit, or reject it. Only then does it execute.

issues.original: There are three ways:
issues.fixed: Here are three ways:

issues.original: Let me show exactly what happens. Say I'm working on an Express API and want to add rate limiting:
issues.fixed: Let me show exactly what happens. Say I'm working on an Express API and want to add rate limiting.

issues.original: Without plan mode, Claude would have just started writing code, and I would have caught these details in review instead of upfront.
issues.fixed: Without plan mode, Claude would have just started writing code, and I would have caught these details in review, not upfront.

issues.original: Skipping plan mode because the task is small. Small tasks often lead to misunderstandings. A one-line fix in the wrong file is worse than a large feature with a clear plan.
issues.fixed: Skipping plan mode for small tasks can lead to misunderstandings. A one-line fix in the wrong file is worse than a large feature with a clear plan.

issues.original: You can steer the plan with instructions:
issues.fixed: You can steer the plan with instructions.

issues.original: The model respects the scope constraint. This keeps plans from becoming 20-step novels.
issues.fixed: The model respects the scope constraint, preventing plans from becoming 20-step novels.

issues.original: A sub-agent is a Claude Code instance spawned by the parent agent to handle a scoped task. It has its own context window, tool set, and instructions. The parent orchestrates, while the sub-agents do the work.
issues.fixed: A sub-agent is a Claude Code instance spawned by the parent agent to handle a scoped task. It has its own context window, tool set, and instructions. The parent orchestrates, and the sub-agents do the work.

issues.original: The sub-agent's context window is separate. The parent's remains clean. This is crucial when working on a complex task and need research without cluttering the main context with 30 file reads.
issues.fixed: The sub-agent's context window is separate, keeping the parent's clean. This is crucial for complex tasks that require research without cluttering the main context with 30 file reads.

issues.original: Spawning a sub-agent is not instant. For a 10-second task, the overhead isn't worth it.
issues.fixed: Spawning a sub-agent takes time. For a 10-second task, the overhead isn't worth it.

issues.original: A git worktree is a second working tree of the same repo, on a different branch, in a different directory. Claude Code knows about them: the [CODE] tool creates a new branch + worktree and switches the session into it.
issues.fixed: A git worktree is a second working tree of the same repo, on a different branch, in a different directory. Claude Code uses the [CODE] tool to create a new branch and worktree, then switches the session into it.

issues.original: When Claude calls [CODE], here is what happens:
issues.fixed: When Claude calls [CODE], this happens:

issues.original: The key insight: the worktree shares the same [CODE] object store as the original repo. Commits, branches, and refs are shared. But the working tree — the files on disk — is independent.
issues.fixed: The key insight: the worktree shares the same [CODE] object store as the original repo. Commits, branches, and refs are shared, but the working tree (the files on disk) is independent.

issues.original: If hooks are how you reach into Claude Code, settings.json is where you tell it what it is allowed to touch in the first place. It is also the file that catches everyone with its precedence rules.
issues.fixed: Hooks let you interact with Claude Code, while settings.json specifies what it can access. This file also confuses many with its precedence rules.

issues.original: The merge rule: later layers override earlier ones, key by key. **Permissions are additive** for [CODE], **subtractive** for [CODE] — once any layer denies something, no other layer can re-allow it. This asymmetry is what makes the system safe.
issues.fixed: Later layers override earlier ones, key by key. Permissions are additive for [CODE] and subtractive for [CODE]. Once a layer denies something, no other layer can re-allow it. This asymmetry ensures the system's safety.

issues.original: Practical consequence: keep org policy in [CODE], keep project rules in [CODE] (committed), keep your 'I trust this exact thing on my machine' overrides in [CODE].
issues.fixed: Practical advice: store org policies in [CODE], project rules in [CODE] (committed), and your personal overrides in [CODE].

issues.original: A bare tool name with no parentheses (e.g., just [CODE]) allows everything for that tool. That is almost always too broad outside [CODE] for trusted personal use.
issues.fixed: A bare tool name without parentheses (e.g., just [CODE]) allows all actions for that tool. This is usually too broad for anything but trusted personal use.

issues.original: Once anything in the merged config denies an action, nothing else can re-allow it. This is the lever you want.
issues.fixed: Once the merged config denies an action, nothing can re-allow it. This is the control you need.

issues.original: An org can commit a [CODE] with deny rules. Individual developers cannot override those denies. This is the mechanism for shared safety policy.
issues.fixed: An organization can commit [CODE] with deny rules. Individual developers cannot override these. This enforces a shared safety policy.
